title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: vignette demonstrates use nmfkc package Supervised Classification, technique referred NMF-LAB (Label-based NMF).

title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: NMF-LAB approach treats multi-class classification matrix factorization task: \[Y \approx X C \] \(Y\) (Target): One-hot encoded matrix class labels (Classes \(\times\) Samples). \(\) (Input): Feature matrix Kernel matrix constructed features. \(X\) (Learned Basis): mapping matrix latent factors class labels. \(C\) (Coefficient/Parameter): Captures relationship input \(\) classes. Key Trade-: Linear Model (\(= Features\)): Highly interpretable. \(C\) tells exactly feature contributes class. Kernel Model (\(= Kernel\)): Highly accurate. Handles non-linear boundaries harder interpret. explore trade-using iris dataset. First, let’s load required packages.
code: library(nmfkc) library(palmerpenguins) #> Warning: package 'palmerpenguins' was built under R version 4.4.2

what: Example 1: The Iris Dataset (Linear vs. Kernel)
title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: goal classify 3 species iris flowers (setosa, versicolor, virginica) based 4 measurements.

previous_headings: Example 1: The Iris Dataset (Linear vs. Kernel)
title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: convert species labels binary class matrix \(Y\) normalize features \(U\).
code: # 1. Prepare Labels (Y) label_iris <- iris$Species Y_iris <- nmfkc.class(label_iris) # One-hot encoding (3 Classes x 150 Samples) rank_iris <- length(unique(label_iris)) # Number of classes  # 2. Prepare Features (U) # Normalize features to [0, 1] range and transpose to (Features x Samples) U_iris <- t(nmfkc.normalize(iris[, -5]))

previous_headings: Example 1: The Iris Dataset (Linear vs. Kernel)
title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: First, fit Linear Model using features \(U\) directly input matrix \(\). allows us see “feature drives class?” inspecting matrix \(C\). Interpretation: Looking matrix \(C\) : Class1 (Setosa): high weights Sepal.Width (2nd col). Class3 (Virginica): high weights Petal.Length (3rd col) Petal.Width (4th col). “White-box” transparency main advantage linear model. Accuracy Check: evaluate model using confusion matrix. (Note: Linear models often struggle overlapping classes like versicolor virginica.)
code: # Use Normalized Features directly as A A_linear <- U_iris  # Fit Linear NMF-LAB res_linear <- nmfkc(Y = Y_iris, A = A_linear, rank = rank_iris, seed = 123, prefix = "Class") #> Y(3,150)~X(3,3)C(3,4)A(4,150)=XB(3,150)...0sec  # --- Interpretability Check --- # The matrix C (Q x R) shows the weight of each Feature (columns) for each Class (rows). # Let's look at the estimated weights: round(res_linear$C, 2) #>        Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width #> Class1         0.00        0.87         0.00        0.00 #> Class2         0.03        0.00         0.57        0.00 #> Class3         0.01        0.00         0.08        0.78 pred_linear <- predict(res_linear, type = "class") (f_linear <- table(fitted.label = pred_linear, label = label_iris)) #>             label #> fitted.label setosa versicolor virginica #>    setosa        50          0         0 #>    virginica      0         50        50  # Calculate Accuracy (assuming diagonal correspondence) acc_linear <- sum(diag(f_linear)) / sum(f_linear) cat(paste0("Linear Model Accuracy: ", round(acc_linear * 100, 2), "%\n")) #> Linear Model Accuracy: 66.67%

previous_headings: Example 1: The Iris Dataset (Linear vs. Kernel)
title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: improve accuracy, switch Kernel Model. map features high-dimensional space using Gaussian kernel. Result: accuracy jumps significantly (often >96%). kernel successfully separates complex boundaries.
code: # 1. Optimize Kernel Width (beta) # Heuristic estimation of beta res_beta <- nmfkc.kernel.beta.nearest.med(U_iris)  # Cross-validation for fine-tuning (using generated candidates) cv_res <- nmfkc.kernel.beta.cv(Y_iris, rank = rank_iris, U = U_iris,                                 beta = res_beta$beta_candidates, plot = FALSE) #> beta=11034.8457794195...0sec #> beta=2377.38545463332...0sec #> beta=512.192169503925...0.1sec #> beta=110.348457794195...0.1sec #> beta=23.7738545463332...0.1sec #> beta=5.12192169503925...0.1sec #> beta=1.10348457794195...0.1sec best_beta <- cv_res$beta  # 2. Fit Kernel NMF-LAB A_kernel <- nmfkc.kernel(U_iris, beta = best_beta) res_kernel <- nmfkc(Y = Y_iris, A = A_kernel, rank = rank_iris, seed = 123, prefix = "Class") #> Y(3,150)~X(3,3)C(3,150)A(150,150)=XB(3,150)...0sec  # 3. Prediction and Evaluation fitted_label <- predict(res_kernel, type = "class") (f_kernel <- table(fitted.label = fitted_label, label = label_iris)) #>             label #> fitted.label setosa versicolor virginica #>   setosa         50          0         0 #>   versicolor      0         49         2 #>   virginica       0          1        48  # Calculate Accuracy acc_kernel <- sum(diag(f_kernel)) / sum(f_kernel) cat(paste0("Kernel Model Accuracy: ", round(acc_kernel * 100, 2), "%\n")) #> Kernel Model Accuracy: 98%

previous_headings: Example 1: The Iris Dataset (Linear vs. Kernel)
title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: Finally, let’s visualize Basis Matrix \(X\) successful kernel model. Ideally, look like diagonal matrix, mapping Latent Factor specific Species.
code: image(t(res_kernel$X)[, nrow(res_kernel$X):1],        main = "Basis Matrix X (Kernel Model)\nMapping Factors to Species",       axes = FALSE, col = hcl.colors(12, "YlOrRd", rev = TRUE))  axis(1, at = seq(0, 1, length.out = rank_iris), labels = colnames(res_kernel$X)) axis(2, at = seq(0, 1, length.out = rank_iris), labels = rev(rownames(res_kernel$X)), las = 2) box()

title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: Let’s apply Kernel NMF-LAB workflow classify penguin species (Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo), focusing Probabilistic (Soft) nature NMF classification.

title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: must remove rows missing values (NA) kernel matrix \(\) handle missing entries input features.
code: # Load and clean data (remove rows with NAs) d_penguins <- na.omit(palmerpenguins::penguins)  # Prepare Y (Labels) label_penguins <- d_penguins$species Y_penguins <- nmfkc.class(label_penguins)  # Prepare U (Features) U_penguins <- t(nmfkc.normalize(d_penguins[, 3:6]))

title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: use heuristic \(\beta\) directly quick demonstration.
code: rank_penguins <- length(unique(label_penguins))  # 1. Heuristic beta estimation best_beta_penguins <- nmfkc.kernel.beta.nearest.med(U_penguins)$beta  # 2. Optimization A_penguins <- nmfkc.kernel(U_penguins, beta = best_beta_penguins) res_penguins <- nmfkc(Y = Y_penguins, A = A_penguins, rank = rank_penguins,                        seed = 123, prefix = "Class") #> Y(3,333)~X(3,3)C(3,333)A(333,333)=XB(3,333)...0.2sec

title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: Unlike many classifiers output final label, NMF provides probability distribution classes. plot shows predicted probability penguin. Solid Blocks Color: Indicate high confidence predictions. Mixed Colors: Indicate samples model uncertain (often boundary species).
code: # Get probabilistic predictions probs <- predict(res_penguins, type = "prob")  # Visualize barplot(probs, col = c("#FF8C00", "#9932CC", "#008B8B"), border = NA,         main = "Soft Classification Probabilities (Penguins)",         xlab = "Sample Index", ylab = "Probability")  legend("topright", legend = levels(label_penguins),         fill = c("#FF8C00", "#9932CC", "#008B8B"), bg = "white", cex = 0.8)

title: Classification with NMF-LAB
text: Finally, calculate accuracy.
code: fitted_label_p <- predict(res_penguins, type = "class") (f_penguins <- table(Predicted = fitted_label_p, Actual = label_penguins)) #>            Actual #> Predicted   Adelie Chinstrap Gentoo #>   Adelie       146         0      0 #>   Chinstrap      0        68      0 #>   Gentoo         0         0    119  acc_p <- sum(diag(f_penguins)) / sum(f_penguins) cat(paste0("Penguins Accuracy: ", round(acc_p * 100, 2), "%\n")) #> Penguins Accuracy: 100%

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: Welcome nmfkc package! vignette provides beginner-friendly introduction core function, nmfkc(). Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) technique decomposes large data matrix \(Y\) two smaller matrices, \(X\) \(B\): \[Y \approx X B\] key feature NMF elements must non-negative (\(\ge 0\)). makes results intuitive, original data can understood additive combination parts. guide, cover: Basic NMF: Extracting latent topics using Movie Ratings example. Interpretation: Understanding decomposed matrices represent. Missing Values: handle predict missing data (e.g., recommendations).

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: understand NMF, let’s imagine scenario 5 Users rating 4 Movies scale 1 5. First, load package.
code: library(nmfkc)

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: create rating matrix Y. dataset contains two hidden genres: “Action” (Movies 1 & 2) “Romance” (Movies 3 & 4).
code: # Rows: Users (U1-U5), Cols: Movies (M1-M4) # U1, U2, U3 prefer Action movies. # U4, U5 prefer Romance movies. Y <- matrix(   c(5, 4, 1, 1,     4, 5, 1, 2,     5, 5, 2, 2,     1, 2, 5, 4,     1, 1, 4, 5),   nrow = 5, byrow = TRUE )  # Assign names for better interpretation rownames(Y) <- paste0("User", 1:5) colnames(Y) <- c("Action1", "Action2", "Romance1", "Romance2")  # Check the data print(Y) #>       Action1 Action2 Romance1 Romance2 #> User1       5       4        1        1 #> User2       4       5        1        2 #> User3       5       5        2        2 #> User4       1       2        5        4 #> User5       1       1        4        5

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: use nmfkc() function decompose matrix. Since assume 2 genres (Action Romance), set rank = 2.
code: # Run NMF with rank = 2 res <- nmfkc(Y, rank = 2, seed = 123) #> Y(5,4)~X(5,2)B(2,4)...0sec

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: NMF decomposes \(Y\) \(X\) (Basis) \(B\) (Coefficient). (Note: order bases may vary depending random seed. example seed=123, Basis 1 corresponds Action Basis 2 Romance.)

previous_headings: 1. Basic Usage: Analyzing Movie Ratings > Interpretation
title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: matrix \(X\) represents “much User likes Genre (Basis).” Basis1: High values User1, User2, User3 (Action fans). Basis2: High values User4 User5 (Romance fans).

previous_headings: 1. Basic Usage: Analyzing Movie Ratings > Interpretation
title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: matrix \(B\) represents “Genre Movie belongs .” Basis1: High weights Action1 Action2. Basis2: High weights Romance1 Romance2. can see, NMF automatically discovered hidden structures (“Action” vs “Romance”) user preferences without explicitly told.
code: # Each row represents a latent factor res$B #>          Action1   Action2  Romance1  Romance2 #> Basis1 14.275611 13.907355  1.264821  2.166817 #> Basis2  1.672713  3.159933 11.781222 11.778951

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: nmfkc provides tools visually diagnose model.

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: Use plot() function check error minimized properly iterations.
code: plot(res, main = "Convergence Plot")

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: nmfkc.residual.plot() function allows compare Original Matrix (\(Y\)), Fitted Matrix (\(XB\)), Residuals (\(E\)) side--side.  middle plot (Fitted Matrix) successfully captures block structure original data.
code: # Visualize Original vs Fitted vs Residuals nmfkc.residual.plot(Y, res)

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: powerful feature nmfkc robustness Missing Values (NA). useful tasks like Recommendation Systems, want predict user rate movie haven’t seen yet.

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: Let’s assume User1 seen Action1 yet. set value NA.
code: Y_missing <- Y Y_missing["User1", "Action1"] <- NA # Introduce missing value print(Y_missing) #>       Action1 Action2 Romance1 Romance2 #> User1      NA       4        1        1 #> User2       4       5        1        2 #> User3       5       5        2        2 #> User4       1       2        5        4 #> User5       1       1        4        5

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: Simply pass matrix NAs nmfkc(). algorithm automatically handles ignoring missing entries optimization.
code: res_na <- nmfkc(Y_missing, rank = 2, seed = 123) #> Notice: Missing values (NA) in Y were treated as weights=0. #> Y(5,4)~X(5,2)B(2,4)...0sec

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: fitted model (\(X \times B\)) provides estimate missing entry. User1 liked Action movies, model predicted reasonably high rating (3.62) missing Action movie, closer actual rating (5) low rating.
code: # Extract the predicted value from the fitted matrix XB predicted_rating <- res_na$XB["User1", "Action1"] actual_rating <- Y["User1", "Action1"] # The original hidden value (5)  cat(paste0("Actual Rating:    ", actual_rating, "\n")) #> Actual Rating:    5 cat(paste0("Predicted Rating: ", round(predicted_rating, 2), "\n")) #> Predicted Rating: 3.62

title: Introduction to nmfkc
text: nmfkc package, can easily: Decompose complex data interpretable parts (\(X\) \(B\)). Handle missing values robustly imputation prediction. Visualize results verify fit. advanced topics, Time Series Analysis Covariate-assisted NMF, please refer vignettes (Topic Modeling Time Series Analysis).

title: Soft community detection in networks with nmfkc.net
text: nmfkc.net() symmetric NMF network (adjacency) matrices. type = "bi" factorises symmetric matrix . row node’s graded membership across communities, soft community detection: unlike hard method (e.g. Louvain), boundary nodes revealed mixed membership rather forced one group. vignette uses self-contained synthetic network known community structure, can check nmfkc.net() recovers nmfkc.net.rank() finds right number communities.
code: library(nmfkc)

what: A synthetic network (stochastic block model, 3 communities)
title: Soft community detection in networks with nmfkc.net
text: draw undirected graph 60 nodes stochastic block model: three communities 20 nodes, high within-community edge probability low -community one. deliberately add three “bridge” nodes (one per community), also wired second community, network contains genuinely mixed nodes. true community node known.
code: set.seed(1) K <- 3; n_each <- 20; N <- K * n_each       # 60 nodes, 3 communities block <- rep(1:K, each = n_each)            # true community labels  p_in <- 0.6; p_out <- 0.05 Prob <- matrix(p_out, N, N) for (k in 1:K) Prob[block == k, block == k] <- p_in Y <- matrix(rbinom(N * N, 1, Prob), N, N)   # 0/1 adjacency Y[lower.tri(Y)] <- t(Y)[lower.tri(Y)]        # make symmetric diag(Y) <- 0  # add three bridge nodes, each also linked to a second community bridge <- c(20, 40, 60); into <- c(2, 3, 1) for (i in seq_along(bridge)) {   b <- bridge[i]; tgt <- which(block == into[i])   e <- rbinom(length(tgt), 1, 0.45)   Y[b, tgt] <- pmax(Y[b, tgt], e); Y[tgt, b] <- Y[b, tgt] }  isSymmetric(Y); sum(Y) / 2                   # symmetric, number of edges #> [1] TRUE #> [1] 437  # adjacency matrix in the original (random) node order -- structure is hidden image(1:N, 1:N, Y[, N:1], col = c("white", "steelblue"),       xlab = "node", ylab = "node", main = "Adjacency (original order)")

title: Soft community detection in networks with nmfkc.net
code: res <- nmfkc.net(Y, rank = 3, type = "bi", nstart = 10, maxit = 500) res$r.squared                 # fit #> [1] 0.4028952 head(round(res$X.prob, 2))    # soft membership: rows = nodes, columns = communities #>      Basis1 Basis2 Basis3 #> [1,]   0.92   0.08   0.00 #> [2,]   0.80   0.16   0.04 #> [3,]   0.88   0.00   0.12 #> [4,]   0.96   0.04   0.00 #> [5,]   0.96   0.04   0.00 #> [6,]   0.86   0.02   0.12 head(res$X.cluster)           # hard label = argmax membership #> [1] 1 1 1 1 1 1

what: Did it recover the known communities?
title: Soft community detection in networks with nmfkc.net
text: cross-tabulation essentially block-diagonal: apart bridge nodes, every node assigned true community. bridge nodes (rows ) roughly 50/50 two communities, hard label simply tips whichever side marginally stronger — exactly situation soft membership informative forced assignment.
code: table(true = block, estimated = res$X.cluster) #>     estimated #> true  1  2  3 #>    1 20  0  0 #>    2  0 20  0 #>    3  1  0 19  # the three bridge nodes have genuinely split memberships round(res$X.prob[bridge, ] * 100, 1) #>      Basis1 Basis2 Basis3 #> [1,]   57.4   41.4    1.1 #> [2,]    0.7   53.4   45.9 #> [3,]   51.1    0.0   48.9

title: Soft community detection in networks with nmfkc.net
text: Re-ordering nodes recovered community turns adjacency matrix clean block-diagonal, membership matrix shows three crisp blocks:
code: ord <- order(res$X.cluster) op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1)) image(1:N, 1:N, Y[ord, rev(ord)], col = c("white", "steelblue"),       xlab = "node (reordered)", ylab = "", main = "Adjacency by community") image(1:N, 1:K, res$X.prob[ord, , drop = FALSE],       col = hcl.colors(20, "Blues", rev = TRUE),       xlab = "node (reordered)", ylab = "community", axes = FALSE,       main = "Soft membership") axis(1); axis(2, at = 1:K) par(op)

what: Network diagram (Graphviz DOT): soft vs. hard
title: Soft community detection in networks with nmfkc.net
text: nmfkc.net.DOT() turns fit Graphviz DOT graph: network node linked community (basis) hubs edges whose thickness encodes membership strength. raise threshold 0.25 substantial memberships drawn — pure node links single hub, lets neato layout pull three communities apart, bridge nodes keep links settle . Rendered suggested package DiagrammeR, embeds directly HTML output. difference two views node colour: Y.cluster = "soft" (default) blends community colours proportion membership, three bridge nodes appear mixed colour. Y.cluster = "hard" paints node single colour dominant community. Soft – three bridge nodes added earlier (bridge <- c(20, 40, 60), wired second community) show blended colour: Hard – bridge nodes (20, 40, 60) forced one community colour: (DiagrammeR installed, diagrams skipped.)
code: dot_soft <- nmfkc.net.DOT(res,   Y.label = as.character(1:N), X.label = paste("Community", 1:K),   Y.title = "Network nodes",   X.title = "Communities",   layout  = "neato", threshold = 0.25, Y.cluster = "soft") DiagrammeR::grViz(as.character(dot_soft)) dot_hard <- nmfkc.net.DOT(res,   Y.label = as.character(1:N), X.label = paste("Community", 1:K),   Y.title = "Network nodes",   X.title = "Communities",   layout  = "neato", threshold = 0.25, Y.cluster = "hard") DiagrammeR::grViz(as.character(dot_hard))

title: Soft community detection in networks with nmfkc.net
text: rank-selection diagnostics nmfkc.rank available symmetric model. recommended rank cross-validation (ECV) minimum; effective rank R-squared elbow corroborate .
code: rk <- nmfkc.net.rank(Y, rank = 1:6, type = "bi", nstart = 5) #> Note: sample-clustering quality (silhouette / CPCC / dist.cor) is not part of rank selection; compute it from a list of fits with nmf.cluster.criteria().  See ?nmf.cluster.criteria rk$rank.best #> [1] 3 round(rk$criteria, 3) #>   rank effective.rank effective.rank.ratio r.squared sigma.ecv #> 1    1             NA                   NA     0.035     0.437 #> 2    2          1.944                0.972     0.229     0.409 #> 3    3          2.999                1.000     0.403     0.373 #> 4    4          3.830                0.958     0.416     0.405 #> 5    5          4.860                0.972     0.437     0.468 #> 6    6          5.918                0.986     0.452     0.500 #>   effective.rank.expected effective.rank.index #> 1                   1.000                   NA #> 2                   1.649                0.840 #> 3                   2.301                0.999 #> 4                   2.955                0.838 #> 5                   3.609                0.900 #> 6                   4.263                0.953

title: Soft community detection in networks with nmfkc.net
text: nmfkc.net() recovers three planted communities core nodes, nmfkc.net.rank() selects three. membership soft (res$X.prob), three bridge nodes sit communities show split membership instead forced one group — practical advantage NMF hard community detection, made visible soft-vs-hard DOT graphs . type = "bi" () usual choice undirected, non-negative network; type = "tri" () adds community-interaction matrix , type = "signed" handles networks negative weights. choosing number communities, advice rank-selection vignette applies: use ECV minimum primary criterion corroborate effective rank. See ?nmfkc.net, ?nmfkc.net.ecv, ?nmfkc.net.rank, companion vignette “Choosing NMF rank data known true rank”.

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: vignette demonstrates NMF-RE (Non-negative Matrix Factorization Random Effects), mixed-effects extension NMF implemented nmfkc package.

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: Standard NMF covariates models data : \[Y \approx X \Theta \] \(\Theta \) captures systematic (fixed) effects covariates \(\) latent scores. However, many applications — longitudinal studies, panel data, clustered observations — individuals exhibit unit-specific deviations explained covariates alone. NMF-RE addresses adding random effects \(U\): \[Y = X(\Theta + U) + \mathcal{E}\] \(Y\) (\(P \times N\)): Non-negative observation matrix (e.g., repeated measurements \(P\) time points \(N\) subjects). \(X\) (\(P \times Q\)): Non-negative basis matrix learned data. \(\Theta\) (\(Q \times K\)): Coefficient matrix covariate effects. \(\) (\(K \times N\)): Covariate matrix (e.g., intercept, sex, treatment). \(U\) (\(Q \times N\)): Random effects matrix capturing individual deviations. \(\mathcal{E}\): Residual noise variance \(\sigma^2\). random effects \(U\) follow ridge-type prior controlled variance \(\tau^2\), penalty \(\lambda = \sigma^2 / \tau^2\) determines degree shrinkage.

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: Automatic regularization: df.rate controls effective degrees freedom consumed \(U\), preventing overfitting. Wild bootstrap inference: Standard errors, z-values, p-values, confidence intervals \(\Theta\) without repeated constrained re-optimization. Trace-based ICC: Quantifies proportion variance attributable random effects.

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: use Orthodont dataset nlme package: orthodontic distance measurements 27 children ages 8, 10, 12, 14. covariate interest sex (Male/Female).
code: library(nmfkc) library(nlme) #> Warning: package 'nlme' was built under R version 4.4.3  data(Orthodont) head(Orthodont) #> Grouped Data: distance ~ age | Subject #>   distance age Subject  Sex #> 1     26.0   8     M01 Male #> 2     25.0  10     M01 Male #> 3     29.0  12     M01 Male #> 4     31.0  14     M01 Male #> 5     21.5   8     M02 Male #> 6     22.5  10     M02 Male

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: column \(Y\) subject, row time point (age). Since 4 ages 27 subjects, \(Y\) \(4 \times 27\).

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: create \(2 \times 27\) covariate matrix intercept row binary male indicator.
code: male <- ifelse(Orthodont$Sex[seq(1, 108, 4)] == "Male", 1, 0) A <- rbind(intercept = 1, male = male) A[, 1:6] #>           [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] [,6] #> intercept    1    1    1    1    1    1 #> male         1    1    1    1    1    1

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: fitting model, use nmfre.dfU.scan() scan different regularization levels. dfU cap rate limits many effective degrees freedom random effects \(U\) can consume, preventing overfitting. scan evaluates rates 0.1 1.0 (1.0 means cap). Key columns output: dfU: Effective degrees freedom actually used. safeguard: Whether dfU stayed cap (TRUE = safe). hit: Whether \(\tau^2\) updated freely (TRUE = unconstrained). ICC: Trace-based Intraclass Correlation Coefficient.
code: scan_result <- nmfre.dfU.scan(1:10/10, Y, A, rank = 1) scan_result #>    rate dfU.cap  dfU safeguard   hit converged  tau2 sigma2    ICC #> 1   0.1     2.7 2.70     FALSE  TRUE      TRUE 0.443      1 0.0270 #> 2   0.2     5.4 5.40     FALSE  TRUE      TRUE 0.996      1 0.0588 #> 3   0.3     8.1 5.42      TRUE FALSE      TRUE 1.000      1 0.0590 #> 4   0.4    10.8 5.42      TRUE FALSE      TRUE 1.000      1 0.0590 #> 5   0.5    13.5 5.42      TRUE FALSE      TRUE 1.000      1 0.0590 #> 6   0.6    16.2 5.42      TRUE FALSE      TRUE 1.000      1 0.0590 #> 7   0.7    18.9 5.42      TRUE FALSE      TRUE 1.000      1 0.0590 #> 8   0.8    21.6 5.42      TRUE FALSE      TRUE 1.000      1 0.0590 #> 9   0.9    24.3 5.42      TRUE FALSE      TRUE 1.000      1 0.0590 #> 10  1.0    27.0 5.42      TRUE FALSE      TRUE 1.000      1 0.0590

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: good df.rate satisfies two criteria: safeguard = TRUE: cap overly binding. hit = TRUE: model converges natural solution without cap forcing artificial penalty. Choose smallest rate conditions hold. balances model flexibility overfitting.

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: Based scan, select df.rate = 0.2 fit model rank = 1 (single latent trend). prefix argument labels basis.
code: res <- nmfre(Y, A, rank = 1, df.rate = 0.2, prefix = "Trend")

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: summary() displays variance components, fit statistics, coefficient table significance stars.
code: summary(res) #> NMF-RE: Y(4,27) = X(4,1) [C(1,2) A + U(1,27)] #> Iterations: 2 (converged, epsilon = 1e-05) #> R-squared (cor^2):    0.5739 (XB+blup), 0.4167 (XB) #> R-squared (uncentered):     0.9937 (XB+blup), 0.9915 (XB) #> R-squared (centered): 0.4139 (XB+blup), 0.2133 (XB) #>  #> Variance components: #>   sigma2 = 1  (residual) #>   tau2   = 0.9961  (random effect) #>   lambda = 1.004  (sigma2 / tau2) #>   ICC    = 0.0588  (tau2*tr(X'X) / (tau2*tr(X'X) + sigma2*P)) #>   dfU    = 5.40 <= 5.40 (cap rate = 0.20) #>  #> Coefficients: #>                   Estimate Std. Error (Boot) z value   Pr(>z)  #> intercept:Trend1    90.393      2.471  2.452   36.58   <2e-16 *** #>      male:Trend1     9.606      3.056  2.977    3.14 0.0008356 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: Variance components: \(\sigma^2\): Residual variance (measurement noise). \(\tau^2\): Random effect variance (individual heterogeneity). ICC: Proportion variance random effects. Higher ICC means stronger individual differences relative noise. Coefficient table (\(\Theta\)): Estimate: Effect covariate latent trend. Std. Error: wild bootstrap. Pr(>|z|): One-sided p-value (H0: \(\Theta_{qk} = 0\) vs H1: \(\Theta_{qk} > 0\)), appropriate \(\Theta\) non-negative. R-squared: \(R^2\) (XB+blup): Fit including fixed random effects. \(R^2\) (XB): Fit fixed effects — gap shows much random effects improve fit.

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: plot growth curves see model captures population-level trends (fixed effects) individual deviations (random effects).
code: age <- c(8, 10, 12, 14)  plot(age, res$XB[, 1], type = "n", ylim = range(Y),      xlab = "Age (years)", ylab = "Distance (mm)",      main = "Orthodont: NMF-RE Growth Curves")  # Plot observed data points for (j in 1:27) {   pch_j <- ifelse(male[j] == 1, 4, 1)   points(age, Y[, j], pch = pch_j, col = "gray60") }  # Plot individual predictions (fixed + random effects) for (j in 1:27) {   lines(age, res$XB.blup[, j], col = "steelblue", lty = 3, lwd = 0.8) }  # Plot population-level fixed effects (two lines: male and female) for (j in 1:27) {   lines(age, res$XB[, j], col = "red", lwd = 2) }  legend("topleft",   legend = c("Fixed effect (male/female)", "Fixed + Random (BLUP)",              "Male (observed)", "Female (observed)"),   lwd = c(2, 1, NA, NA), lty = c(1, 3, NA, NA),   pch = c(NA, NA, 4, 1),   col = c("red", "steelblue", "gray60", "gray60"),   cex = 0.85)

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: Red solid lines: Population-level fitted values fixed effects (\(X \Theta \)). two distinct curves — one males (higher) one females (lower) — reflecting sex effect \(\Theta\). Blue dashed lines: Individual-level predictions (\(X(\Theta + U)\)). line shifted population curve subject’s random effect \(U\), capturing personal growth trajectories. Gray points: Observed measurements. Crosses (\(\times\)) males, circles (\(\circ\)) females.

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: basis \(X\) represents latent temporal pattern shared across subjects. Since rank = 1, one basis vector. shape shows latent factor manifests across four ages. column normalized sum 1.
code: cat("Basis X (temporal pattern):\n") #> Basis X (temporal pattern): print(round(res$X, 4)) #>        Trend1 #> Age 8  0.2308 #> Age 10 0.2409 #> Age 12 0.2566 #> Age 14 0.2717

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: \(\Theta\) maps covariates latent scores: intercept: Baseline level latent trend females. male: Additional contribution males. significant positive value indicates males higher orthodontic distances average.
code: cat("Coefficient matrix (Theta):\n") #> Coefficient matrix (Theta): print(round(res$C, 4)) #>        intercept   male #> Trend1   90.3934 9.6059

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: \(U\) captures individual deviations. Subjects positive \(U\) values grow faster population average; negative values indicate slower growth.
code: barplot(res$U[1, ], names.arg = colnames(Y),         las = 2, cex.names = 0.7,         col = ifelse(male == 1, "steelblue", "salmon"),         main = "Random Effects (U) by Subject",         ylab = "Random effect value") legend("topright", fill = c("steelblue", "salmon"),        legend = c("Male", "Female"), cex = 0.85)

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: fitting NMF-RE, can separate optimization statistical inference. First fit wild.bootstrap = FALSE (faster), run inference separately:
code: # Fit without bootstrap (fast) res_fast <- nmfre(Y, A, rank = 1, df.rate = 0.2, prefix = "Trend",                   wild.bootstrap = FALSE)  # Run inference separately res_inf <- nmfre.inference(res_fast, Y, A, wild.B = 500) res_inf$coefficients[, c("Basis", "Covariate", "Estimate", "SE", "p_value")] #>    Basis Covariate  Estimate       SE       p_value #> 1 Trend1 intercept 90.393445 2.471312 3.306544e-293 #> 2 Trend1      male  9.605879 3.056105  8.356231e-04

code: dot <- nmfkc.DOT(res_inf, type = "YXA", sig.level = 0.05) plot(dot)

title: NMF-RE: Mixed-Effects Modeling with nmfkc
text: NMF-RE provides principled way model individual heterogeneity NMF: use NMF-RE: Longitudinal repeated-measures data subjects differ systematically. Panel data unit-level heterogeneity. NMF application fixed covariates alone leave structured residual variation. details underlying methodology, see: Satoh, K. (2026). Wild Bootstrap Inference Non-Negative Matrix Factorization Random Effects. arXiv:2603.01468. https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01468

title: NMF-FFB with nmfkc
text: vignette demonstrates fit NMF-FFB (Nonnegative Matrix Factorization Feed-Forward + Feedback; formerly NMF-SEM) implemented nmfkc package. NMF-FFB extends standard nonnegative matrix factorization introducing feed-forward exogenous loading feedback latent space. Observed endogenous variables \(Y_1\) approximated \[ Y_1 \approx X (\Theta_1 Y_1 + \Theta_2 Y_2). \] feedback operator \(XC_1\) stable, model implies equilibrium mapping \[ \hat Y_1 \approx (- X\Theta_1)^{-1} X\Theta_2 Y_2 \equiv M_{\mathrm{model}} Y_2. \]

title: NMF-FFB with nmfkc
code: library(lavaan) #> Warning: package 'lavaan' was built under R version 4.4.2 #> This is lavaan 0.6-19 #> lavaan is FREE software! Please report any bugs. data(HolzingerSwineford1939)  d <- HolzingerSwineford1939 index <- complete.cases(d) d <- d[index, ]

title: NMF-FFB with nmfkc
code: d$age.rev <- -(d$ageyr + d$agemo / 12) d$sex.2 <- ifelse(d$sex == 2, 1, 0) d$school.GW <- ifelse(d$school == "Grant-White", 1, 0) d[, c("id", "sex", "ageyr", "agemo", "school", "grade")] <- NULL

title: NMF-FFB with nmfkc
code: library(nmfkc) d <- nmfkc::nmfkc.normalize(d)

what: 2. Split into endogenous and exogenous blocks
title: NMF-FFB with nmfkc
code: exogenous_vars <- c("age.rev", "sex.2", "school.GW") endogenous_vars <- setdiff(colnames(d), exogenous_vars)  Y1 <- t(d[, endogenous_vars]) Y2 <- t(d[, exogenous_vars])

title: NMF-FFB with nmfkc
code: myepsilon <- 1e-6 Q0 <- 3  res0 <- nmfkc(   Y = Y1,   A = Y2,   rank = Q0,   epsilon = myepsilon,   X.L2.ortho = 100 ) #> Y(9,300)~X(9,3)C(3,3)A(3,300)=XB(3,300)...0.1sec  M.simple <- res0$X %*% res0$C

title: NMF-FFB with nmfkc
code: grid_params <- expand.grid(     C1.L1     = c(0,1:9/10,1:10),     C2.L1     = c(0,1:9/10,1:10) ) n_iter <- nrow(grid_params) mae.cv <- 0*1:n_iter  for(i in 1:n_iter){   if (i %% round(n_iter / 10) == 0) {     message(sprintf("Processing... %d%% (%d/%d)", round(i/n_iter*100), i, n_iter))    }     p <- grid_params[i, ]   res.cv <- nmf.ffb.cv(Y1, Y2, rank = Q0,                        X.init = res0$X,                        X.L2.ortho = 100,                        C1.L1 = p$C1.L1,                        C2.L1 = p$C2.L1,                        seed = 1, epsilon = myepsilon)   mae.cv[i] <- res.cv }  f <- data.frame(grid_params,mae.cv) f <- f[order(f$mae.cv),] head(f,5) #    C2.L2.off C1.L1 C2.L1    mae.cv #140         0    10   0.6 0.1820841 #160         0    10   0.7 0.1820843 #180         0    10   0.8 0.1820877 #200         0    10   0.9 0.1820907 #120         0    10   0.5 0.1820908 print(p <- f[1,])

title: NMF-FFB with nmfkc
code: p <- list(C1.L1 = 10, C2.L1 = 0.6)  res <- nmf.ffb(   Y1, Y2,   rank = Q0,   X.init = res0$X,   X.L2.ortho = 100,   C1.L1 = p$C1.L1,   C2.L1 = p$C2.L1,   epsilon = myepsilon ) plot(res$objfunc.full, type = "l",      main = "Objective Function",      ylab = "Loss", xlab = "Iteration")

SC.map <- cor(as.vector(res$M.model),               as.vector(M.simple))  cat("Q=     ", Q0, "

title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
text: Choosing number basis vectors (rank Q) central tuning problem NMF. nmfkc offers several rank-selection tools, based different principle: Since single “best” criterion, vignette applies synthetic dataset whose true rank known, can see ones recover .
code: library(nmfkc)

title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
text: build non-negative 24 x 60 matrix three feature blocks three sample clusters (20 samples ), add noise. construction true rank 3.
code: set.seed(123) r_true <- 3 P <- 24                       # features n_each <- 20                  # samples per cluster N <- r_true * n_each          # 60 samples  # basis: each latent factor is a distinct block of features X <- matrix(0, P, r_true) X[1:8, 1] <- 1; X[9:16, 2] <- 1; X[17:24, 3] <- 1 X <- X + matrix(runif(P * r_true, 0, 0.1), P, r_true)   # small background  # coefficients: each sample loads mainly on its own cluster's factor cl_true <- rep(1:r_true, each = n_each) B <- matrix(runif(r_true * N, 0, 0.2), r_true, N) for (j in seq_len(N)) B[cl_true[j], j] <- runif(1, 1, 2)  Y <- X %*% B Y <- Y + matrix(rnorm(P * N, 0, 0.15 * mean(X %*% B)), P, N)  # noise Y[Y < 0] <- 0  dim(Y) #> [1] 24 60 image(1:N, 1:P, t(Y), xlab = "sample", ylab = "feature",       main = "Synthetic data (true rank = 3)")

title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
text: nmfkc.rank() fits rank draws three curves one plot: R-squared (red, “Best (Elbow)” marker), broken-stick-corrected effective rank index (green, shown context), element-wise ECV error (blue, “Best (Min)” marker). recommended rank ECV minimum (R-squared elbow).
code: rk <- nmfkc.rank(Y, rank = 1:6) #> Y(24,60)~X(24,1)B(1,60)...0sec #> Y(24,60)~X(24,2)B(2,60)...0sec #> Y(24,60)~X(24,3)B(3,60)...0sec #> Y(24,60)~X(24,4)B(4,60)...0sec #> Y(24,60)~X(24,5)B(5,60)...0sec #> Y(24,60)~X(24,6)B(6,60)...0sec #> Running Element-wise CV (this may take time)... #> Performing Element-wise CV for Q = 1,2,3,4,5,6 (5-fold)... #> Note: sample-clustering quality (silhouette / CPCC / dist.cor) is not part of rank selection; compute it from a list of fits with nmf.cluster.criteria().  See ?nmf.cluster.criteria rk$rank.best                       # recommended rank (ECV minimum) #> [1] 3 round(rk$criteria, 3) #>   rank effective.rank effective.rank.ratio r.squared sigma.ecv #> 1    1             NA                   NA     0.044     0.710 #> 2    2          2.000                1.000     0.549     0.520 #> 3    3          2.988                0.996     0.984     0.106 #> 4    4          3.438                0.860     0.985     0.113 #> 5    5          3.440                0.688     0.986     0.120 #> 6    6          4.587                0.764     0.987     0.132 #>   effective.rank.expected effective.rank.index #> 1                   1.000                   NA #> 2                   1.649                0.999 #> 3                   2.301                0.982 #> 4                   2.955                0.462 #> 5                   3.609                0.000 #> 6                   4.263                0.186

title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
text: predictive engines return held-error per rank; recommended rank minimises . nmfkc.ecv holds scattered entries (Wold’s CV); nmfkc.bicv holds block rows columns (Owen & Perry).
code: ev <- nmfkc.ecv (Y, rank = 1:6) #> Performing Element-wise CV for Q = 1,2,3,4,5,6 (5-fold)... bv <- nmfkc.bicv(Y, rank = 1:6)   # nfolds = 2 (Owen & Perry) by default #> bi-CV: ranks 1,2,3,4,5,6, 2x2 fold grid (Owen-Perry 2009)... data.frame(rank = 1:6,            sigma.ecv  = round(ev$sigma, 3),            sigma.bicv = round(bv$sigma, 3)) #>     rank sigma.ecv sigma.bicv #> Q=1    1     0.710      0.725 #> Q=2    2     0.520      0.531 #> Q=3    3     0.106      0.110 #> Q=4    4     0.113      0.111 #> Q=5    5     0.120      0.111 #> Q=6    6     0.132      0.111 cat("ECV  picks rank", which.min(ev$sigma),     "| bi-CV picks rank", bv$rank[which.min(bv$sigma)], "\n") #> ECV  picks rank 3 | bi-CV picks rank 3

title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
text: rank, NMF run many times random initialisations reproducibility sample clustering summarised cophenetic (Brunet), dispersion (Kim & Park; higher = crisper) pac (Senbabaoglu; lower = less ambiguous).  panel consensus matrix one rank, reordered hierarchical clustering (blue = 0 / different cluster, red = 1 / cluster):  striking point: consensus stays essentially three crisp blocks even ranks 4–6. surplus basis vectors create new reproducible clusters — ranks 4–5 stay near-empty (hard clustering still uses three factors), rank 6 cluster splits erratically across restarts, averages (just intermediate, pink entries, small drop dispersion / rise PAC). consensus reveals genuine stable structure (3) robustly, even rank -specified — useful property. (Use plot(cs, type = "heatmap", rank = 3) enlarge single rank sample labels.)
code: cs <- nmfkc.consensus(Y, rank = 2:6, nrun = 20, keep.consensus = TRUE) #> consensus: ranks 2,3,4,5,6, 20 runs/rank (Brunet 2004); 100 fits total... cs #> Consensus rank selection (Brunet 2004), 20 runs/rank #>   best: dispersion max = rank 3 | PAC min = rank 2 #>  rank cophenetic dispersion   pac #>     2      0.999      0.916 0.000 #>     3      1.000      1.000 0.000 #>     4      1.000      0.968 0.009 #>     5      1.000      0.956 0.016 #>     6      0.999      0.904 0.081 plot(cs)                               # stability curves plot(cs, type = "heatmap")   # all ranks

title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
text: ARD fits NMF -complete rank prunes unneeded components automatically; relevance bar plot read like scree plot (look knee). sensitive point estimate, aggregate several random restarts (nrun).
code: ar <- nmfkc.ard(Y, rank = 8, nrun = 20)   # >=10-20 restarts for a stable mode ar                                        # see "rank over runs" for confidence #> ARD-NMF rank determination (Tan-Fevotte 2013, L2 prior, 20 runs) #>   starting rank: 8  ->  estimated rank (mode): 3 #>   rank over runs: 3:20 (median 3) #>   relevance (representative run): 1 0.989 0.875 0 0 0 0 0 plot(ar)

what: Summary: did they recover the true rank?
title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
code: data.frame(   method = c("nmfkc.rank (ECV)", "nmfkc.ecv", "nmfkc.bicv",              "nmfkc.consensus (dispersion)", "nmfkc.consensus (PAC)",              "nmfkc.ard"),   estimate = c(rk$rank.best,                which.min(ev$sigma),                bv$rank[which.min(bv$sigma)],                cs$rank[which.max(cs$dispersion)],                cs$rank[which.min(cs$pac)],                ar$rank),   true = r_true ) #>                         method estimate true #> 1             nmfkc.rank (ECV)        3    3 #> 2                    nmfkc.ecv        3    3 #> 3                   nmfkc.bicv        3    3 #> 4 nmfkc.consensus (dispersion)        3    3 #> 5        nmfkc.consensus (PAC)        2    3 #> 6                    nmfkc.ard        3    3

what: How the clustering evolves with rank
title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
text: complementary view: nmf.cluster.flow() draws Sankey / alluvial diagram hard sample clustering changes rank grows. fit ranks 1:6 (list index equals rank) colour flows clustering true rank, reference = 3. adjacent-rank ARI (agreement) printed along top.  rank = 1 60 samples form single group; rank = 3 split cleanly three true clusters (20 ); beyond groups -split (ARI next rank starts fall) — visual confirmation 3 right number basis vectors .
code: fits <- lapply(1:6, function(q) nmfkc(Y, Q = q, print.dims = FALSE)) fl <- nmf.cluster.flow(fits, reference = 3) head(fl$clusters)      # rows = individuals, columns = rank, entries = cluster id #>    1 2 3 4 5 6 #> i1 1 1 1 2 2 3 #> i2 1 1 1 2 3 2 #> i3 1 1 1 1 1 1 #> i4 1 1 1 1 1 1 #> i5 1 1 1 1 1 1 #> i6 1 1 1 1 3 2

title: Choosing the NMF rank on data with a known true rank
text: clean dataset clear rank-3 structure, almost every criterion recovers true rank: predictive cross-validations (nmfkc.ecv, nmfkc.bicv), integrated nmfkc.rank, consensus dispersion (crisp three-block consensus matrix) ARD (every nrun restart agrees 3, relevance dropping zero third component) give 3. PAC leans coarser rank 2 — small hint low-rank bias stability measures. real data criteria often disagree much : stability/consensus tends favour low (coarse) ranks, ARD’s count can track starting rank energy spectrum decays smoothly (clear knee). robust practical recipe triangulate: use ECV predictive upper bound, effective-rank / relevance knee lower bound, consensus heatmap confirm interpretable block structure — choose one rank purpose domain knowledge. See ?nmfkc.rank, ?nmfkc.ecv, ?nmfkc.bicv, ?nmfkc.consensus ?nmfkc.ard (cross-referenced) details.

title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: vignette demonstrates apply nmfkc package time series data using NMF-VAR (Non-negative Matrix Factorization - Vector Autoregression) framework. approach models coefficient matrix NMF VAR process (\(B \approx C A_{lag}\)), allowing dimensionality reduction temporal modeling. Key functions nmfkc nmfkc.ar nmfkc.ar.degree.cv directly support R’s native ts objects, preserving time series properties automatically. cover two scenarios: Univariate AR: Forecasting airline passengers (AirPassengers). Multivariate VAR: Analyzing macroeconomic indicators (Canada). First, let’s load necessary packages.
code: library(nmfkc) library(vars) # For Canada dataset #> Warning: package 'vars' was built under R version 4.4.3 #> Loading required package: MASS #> Warning: package 'MASS' was built under R version 4.4.3 #> Loading required package: strucchange #> Warning: package 'strucchange' was built under R version 4.4.2 #> Loading required package: zoo #> Warning: package 'zoo' was built under R version 4.4.3 #>  #> Attaching package: 'zoo' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     as.Date, as.Date.numeric #> Loading required package: sandwich #> Warning: package 'sandwich' was built under R version 4.4.2 #> Loading required package: urca #> Warning: package 'urca' was built under R version 4.4.3 #> Loading required package: lmtest #> Warning: package 'lmtest' was built under R version 4.4.2

title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: AirPassengers dataset contains monthly international airline passenger numbers. model univariate time series using autoregressive (AR) model.

title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: simply log-transform ts object stabilize variance. need manually convert matrix create time vectors; nmfkc functions handle ts objects directly.
code: # Load and transform the ts object d_air <- AirPassengers d_air_log <- log10(d_air) # Still a ts object

title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: use Cross-Validation find optimal degree (lag order). passing ts object d_air_log, function automatically handles time dimension.
code: # Evaluate lag orders from 1 to 14 # Note: ts objects are automatically transposed to (Variables x Time) internally cv_res <- nmfkc.ar.degree.cv(d_air_log, rank = 1, degree = 1:14, epsilon=1e-6, maxit=500000) #> degree=1...0.2sec #> degree=2...0.7sec #> degree=3...0.7sec #> degree=4...0.7sec #> degree=5...0.8sec #> degree=6...0.7sec #> degree=7...0.8sec #> degree=8...0.8sec #> degree=9...0.9sec #> degree=10...0.8sec #> degree=11...0.8sec #> degree=12...0.8sec #> degree=13...0.9sec #> degree=14...1sec # Check the optimal degree cv_res$degree #> [1] 12  # For this example, we will proceed with D=12 (capturing monthly seasonality) D <- 12

title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: construct observation matrix Y covariate matrix (lagged Y) using nmfkc.ar(). returned matrices Y time-based column names derived ts object.
code: # Create matrices for the AR(12) model a_air <- nmfkc.ar(d_air_log, degree = D, intercept = TRUE)  # Fit the NMF-AR model (Rank=1 for univariate) res_air <- nmfkc(Y = a_air$Y, A = a_air$A, rank = 1, epsilon = 1e-6, maxit=500000) #> Y(1,132)~X(1,1)C(1,13)A(13,132)=XB(1,132)...0.2sec  # Check goodness of fit res_air$r.squared #> [1] 0.9798257  # Check for stationarity (spectral radius < 1) nmfkc.ar.stationarity(res_air) #> $spectral.radius #> [1] 0.9983758 #>  #> $stationary #> [1] TRUE

title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: can forecast future values using fitted model. nmfkc.ar.predict uses time properties stored model generate correct future time sequence.
code: # Forecast next 2 years (24 months) h <- 24 pred_res <- nmfkc.ar.predict(x = res_air, Y = a_air$Y, n.ahead = h)  # Convert predictions back to original scale pred_val <- 10^as.vector(pred_res$pred) pred_time <- pred_res$time # Future time points generated by the function  # --- Plotting --- # Setup plot range xlim_range <- range(c(time(d_air), pred_time)) ylim_range <- range(c(d_air, pred_val))  # 1. Observed data (Black) plot(d_air, type = "l", col = "black",       xlim = xlim_range, ylim = ylim_range, lwd = 1,      xlab = "Year", ylab = "Air Passengers", main = "NMF-VAR Forecast (h=24)")  # 2. Fitted values during training (Red) # a_air$Y has column names as time strings; we parse them for plotting fitted_time <- as.numeric(colnames(res_air$XB)) lines(fitted_time, 10^as.vector(res_air$XB), col = "red", lwd = 2)  # 3. Forecast (Blue) # Connect the last observed point to the first forecast for a continuous line last_t <- tail(as.numeric(time(d_air)), 1) last_y <- tail(as.vector(d_air), 1) lines(c(last_t, pred_time), c(last_y, pred_val), col = "blue", lwd = 2, lty = 2)  # Add legend legend("topleft", legend = c("Observed", "Fitted", "Forecast"),        col = c("black", "red", "blue"), lty = c(1, 1, 2), lwd = 2)

what: Example 2: Vector Autoregression with Canada Dataset
title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: Canada dataset contains four quarterly macroeconomic variables. ’ll use multivariate NMF-VAR model analyze relationship variables.

previous_headings: Example 2: Vector Autoregression with Canada Dataset
title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: take first difference achieve stationarity, normalize data \([0, 1]\), transpose Variables x Time format required NMF.
code: # Load, difference, and normalize d0_canada <- Canada dd_canada <- apply(d0_canada, 2, diff) # Returns a matrix (Time x Vars) dn_canada <- nmfkc.normalize(dd_canada)  # Transpose to (Variables x Time) for NMF Y0_canada <- t(dn_canada)  # Create matrices for VAR(1) a_canada <- nmfkc.ar(Y0_canada, degree = 1, intercept = TRUE)

previous_headings: Example 2: Vector Autoregression with Canada Dataset
title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: fit model rank = 2 identify two latent economic conditions driving four variables.
code: # Fit the NMF-VAR model res_canada <- nmfkc(Y = a_canada$Y, A = a_canada$A, rank = 2, prefix = "Condition", epsilon = 1e-6) #> Y(4,82)~X(4,2)C(2,5)A(5,82)=XB(2,82)...0sec  # R-squared and Stationarity res_canada$r.squared #> [1] 0.5994663 nmfkc.ar.stationarity(res_canada) #> $spectral.radius #> [1] 0.7781102 #>  #> $stationary #> [1] TRUE

previous_headings: Example 2: Vector Autoregression with Canada Dataset
title: Time Series Analysis with NMF-VAR
text: can visualize two latent conditions change time.  Finally, can generate DOT script visualize Granger causality (relationships) variables inferred model.
code: # Visualize soft clustering of time trends barplot(res_canada$B.prob, col = c(2, 3), border = NA,         main = "Soft Clustering of Economic Conditions",         xlab = "Time", ylab = "Probability",         names.arg = colnames(a_canada$Y), las=2, cex.names = 0.5) legend("topright", legend = colnames(res_canada$X), fill = c(2, 3), bg = "white") # Generate DOT script for graph visualization dot_script <- nmfkc.ar.DOT(res_canada, intercept = TRUE, threshold=0.01)  # plot(dot_script)  # requires DiagrammeR

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) powerful technique topic modeling. decomposing document-term matrix, can simultaneously discover latent Topics (clusters words) Trends (proportions documents). vignette demonstrates use nmfkc package analyze U.S. presidential inaugural addresses using quanteda package. cover: Data Preparation: Converting text data matrix format suitable nmfkc. Rank Selection: Determining optimal number topics using robust Cross-Validation. Standard NMF: Extracting basic topics interpreting key words. Kernel NMF: Modeling temporal evolution topics using “Year” covariate. First, let’s load necessary packages.
code: library(nmfkc) library(quanteda) #> Package version: 4.1.0 #> Unicode version: 15.1 #> ICU version: 74.1 #> Parallel computing: 32 of 32 threads used. #> See https://quanteda.io for tutorials and examples.

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: create Document-Feature Matrix (DFM) rows represent documents columns represent words.
code: # Load the corpus from quanteda corp <- corpus(data_corpus_inaugural)  # Preprocessing: tokenize, remove stopwords, and punctuation tok <- tokens(corp, remove_punct = TRUE) tok <- tokens_remove(tok, pattern = stopwords("en", source = "snowball"))  # Create DFM and filter df <- dfm(tok) df <- dfm_select(df, min_nchar = 3) # Remove short words (<= 2 chars) df <- dfm_trim(df, min_termfreq = 100) # Remove rare words (appearing < 100 times)  # --- CRITICAL STEP --- # quanteda's DFM is (Documents x Words). # nmfkc expects (Words x Documents). # We must transpose the matrix. d <- as.matrix(df) # Sort by frequency index <- order(colSums(d), decreasing=TRUE)  d <- d[,index] Y <- t(d)  dim(Y) # Features (Words) x Samples (Documents) #> [1] 67 59

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: fitting model, need decide number topics (\(rank\)). nmfkc.rank() function helps us choose appropriate rank. default detail = "full" performs Element-wise Cross-Validation (Wold’s CV). method randomly holds individual matrix elements evaluates well model predicts , providing robust measure rank selection (though takes computation time).  Looking diagnostics (minimum ECV Sigma elbow R-squared curve; green eff.rank line shown context), let’s assume Rank = 3 reasonable choice overview.
code: # Evaluate ranks from 2 to 6 nmfkc.rank(Y, rank = 2:6) #> Y(67,59)~X(67,2)B(2,59)...0sec #> Y(67,59)~X(67,3)B(3,59)...0sec #> Y(67,59)~X(67,4)B(4,59)...0sec #> Y(67,59)~X(67,5)B(5,59)...0sec #> Y(67,59)~X(67,6)B(6,59)...0sec #> Running Element-wise CV (this may take time)... #> Performing Element-wise CV for Q = 2,3,4,5,6 (5-fold)... #> Note: sample-clustering quality (silhouette / CPCC / dist.cor) is not part of rank selection; compute it from a list of fits with nmf.cluster.criteria().  See ?nmf.cluster.criteria

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: fit standard NMF model (\(Y \approx XB\)) rank = 3. context topic modeling: \(X\) (Basis Matrix): Represents Topics (distribution words). \(B\) (Coefficient Matrix): Represents Trends (distribution topics across documents).
code: rank <- 3 # Set seed for reproducibility res_std <- nmfkc(Y, rank = rank, seed = 123, prefix = "Topic") #> Y(67,59)~X(67,3)B(3,59)...0sec  # Check Goodness of Fit (R-squared) res_std$r.squared #> [1] 0.6129089

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: can identify meaning topic looking words highest weights basis matrix X. (Note: Interpretation depends result. example, one topic might contain words like “government, people, states” (Political), another might “world, peace, freedom” (International).)

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: One unique features nmfkc Kernel NMF. standard NMF, order documents ignored; speech treated independently. However, inaugural addresses strong temporal component. using “Year” covariate, can smooth topic proportions time see historical shifts.

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: construct covariate matrix U using year address. find optimal kernel bandwidth (beta) using Cross-Validation.
code: # Covariate: Year of the address years <- as.numeric(substring(names(data_corpus_inaugural), 1, 4)) U <- t(as.matrix(years))  # Optimize beta (Gaussian Kernel width) # We test a specific range of betas to locate the minimum CV error. beta_candidates <- c(0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5) / 10000  # Run CV to find the best beta # Note: We use the same rank (rank=3) as selected above. cv_res <- nmfkc.kernel.beta.cv(Y, rank = rank, U = U, beta = beta_candidates, plot = FALSE) #> beta=2e-05...0.1sec #> beta=5e-05...0sec #> beta=1e-04...0.1sec #> beta=2e-04...0sec #> beta=5e-04...0sec best_beta <- cv_res$beta print(best_beta) #> [1] 2e-04

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: Now fit model using kernel matrix . enforces documents close time (similar years) similar topic distributions.
code: # Create Kernel Matrix A <- nmfkc.kernel(U, beta = best_beta)  # Fit NMF with Kernel Covariates res_ker <- nmfkc(Y, A = A, rank = rank, seed = 123, prefix = "Topic") #> Y(67,59)~X(67,3)C(3,59)A(59,59)=XB(3,59)...0sec

title: Topic Modeling with nmfkc
text: Let’s compare topic proportions change time. Standard NMF (Top): Shows noisy fluctuations. captures specific content speech misses larger historical context. Kernel NMF (Bottom): Reveals smooth historical trends, showing themes like “Nation Building” vs “Global Affairs” evolved centuries.
code: oldpar <- par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))  # Prepare Axis Labels (Rounded to integers) at_points <- seq(1, ncol(Y), length.out = 10) labels_years <- round(seq(min(years), max(years), length.out = 10))  # 1. Standard NMF (Noisy) barplot(res_std$B.prob, col = 2:(rank+1), border = NA, xaxt='n',         main = "Standard NMF: Topic Proportions (Noisy)", ylab = "Probability") axis(1, at = at_points, labels = labels_years)  # 2. Kernel NMF (Smooth trend) barplot(res_ker$B.prob, col = 2:(rank+1), border = NA, xaxt='n',         main = "Kernel NMF: Temporal Topic Evolution (Smooth)", ylab = "Probability") axis(1, at = at_points, labels = labels_years)  # Legend legend("topright", legend = paste("Topic", 1:rank), fill = 2:(rank+1), bg="white", cex=0.8) par(oldpar)

what: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
text: nmfkc R package extends Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) incorporating covariates using kernel methods. supports advanced features like rank selection via cross-validation, time-series modeling (NMF-VAR), supervised classification (NMF-LAB), feed-forward + feedback structural modeling equilibrium interpretation (NMF-FFB; formerly NMF-SEM), mixed-effects modeling random effects (NMF-RE).

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
code: # Stable version (CRAN) install.packages("nmfkc")  # Development version (GitHub, may be unstable) # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("ksatohds/nmfkc@develop")  library(nmfkc)

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
code: browseVignettes("nmfkc") ls("package:nmfkc") ?nmfkc

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
code: citation("nmfkc")

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
text: See browseVignettes("nmfkc") detailed examples covering rank selection, kernel NMF, time-series, classification, NMF-FFB, NMF-RE.
code: library(nmfkc)  # Decompose a matrix Y into basis X and coefficient B with rank = 2 X_true <- cbind(c(1, 0, 1), c(0, 1, 0)) B_true <- cbind(c(1, 0), c(0, 1), c(1, 1)) Y <- X_true %*% B_true  res <- nmfkc(Y, rank = 2, epsilon = 1e-6) plot(res)     # Convergence plot summary(res)  # Summary statistics

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
text: nmfkc package builds upon standard NMF framework incorporating external information (covariates): \[Y(P,N) \approx X(P,Q) \times C(Q,R) \times (R,N)\] \(Y\): Observation matrix (\(P\) features × \(N\) samples) \(\): Covariate matrix (\(R\) covariates × \(N\) samples); defaults identity (standard NMF) \(X\): Basis matrix — learned latent patterns \(C\): Parameter matrix — links covariates latent structure

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
text: NMF-RE: Adds unit-specific random effects \(U\): \(Y = X(\Theta + U) + \mathcal{E}\), estimated via ridge-type BLUP wild bootstrap inference. NMF-FFB (formerly NMF-SEM): Models feed-forward + feedback structure \(Y_1 \approx X(\Theta_1 Y_1 + \Theta_2 Y_2)\), equilibrium mapping \((- X\Theta_1)^{-1} X\Theta_2\).

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization with Kernel Covariates
text: S3 methods coef(), fitted(), residuals(), plot(), summary(), predict() available model classes. See ?nmfkc browseVignettes("nmfkc") full function list.

Sample-clustering quality across ranks — nmf.cluster.criteria
text: Computes clustering-quality criteria silhouette, CPCC, dist.cor list models fitted different ranks (single fit), returning one row per rank.  clustering-stability diagnostics (decisively faithfully samples cluster), conceptually separate rank-selection *.rank functions (use r.squared, effective rank, ECV) complementary nmf.cluster.flow (shows hard clustering changes across ranks). Hard sample clustering requires non-negative coefficient/score matrix (columns form membership simplex); model's coefficient signed (e.g.\ nmfkc.signed, nmfae.signed, nmfre fits whose coefficient negative entries) hard-label silhouette NA distance-based CPCC dist.cor still computed.

title: Sample-clustering quality across ranks — nmf.cluster.criteria
code: nmf.cluster.criteria(fits, Y, Y2 = NULL, names = NULL, plot = TRUE, ...)

title: Sample-clustering quality across ranks — nmf.cluster.criteria
text: fits list fitted models, one per rank, \(N\) individuals (single fitted model also accepted wrapped automatically).  Supported families: nmfkc, nmfkc.signed, nmfae, nmfae.signed, nmfkc.net, nmfre, nmf.sem / nmf.ffb. Y original data matrix used fit models (\(Y_1\) nmf.ffb); required data-space distances. Y2 Exogenous block, required nmf.ffb / nmf.sem. names Optional character vector (length length(fits)) x-axis tick labels.  Defaults result's $rank. plot Logical; draw diagnostics plot immediately (default TRUE); see plot.nmf.cluster.criteria. ... plot = TRUE, graphical arguments forwarded plot.nmf.cluster.criteria.

title: Sample-clustering quality across ranks — nmf.cluster.criteria
text: object class "nmf.cluster.criteria" (returned invisibly): list criteria (data frame one row per result columns rank, silhouette, CPCC, dist.cor, hard) labels (x-axis labels).  Results kept given order (sorted).

title: Sample-clustering quality across ranks — nmf.cluster.criteria
code: # \donttest{ Y <- t(as.matrix(iris[, 1:4])) fits <- lapply(2:6, function(q) nmfkc(Y, Q = q, print.dims = FALSE)) cc <- nmf.cluster.criteria(fits, Y, plot = FALSE) cc$criteria #>   rank silhouette      CPCC  dist.cor hard #> 1    2 0.68578817 0.9064673 0.9746472 TRUE #> 2    3 0.34406544 0.8711792 0.9489567 TRUE #> 3    4 0.12644452 0.8666824 0.9464434 TRUE #> 4    5 0.12848073 0.8611024 0.9508510 TRUE #> 5    6 0.08304005 0.8619749 0.9472392 TRUE plot(cc)  # }

what: Cluster-flow (alluvial) diagram across a sequence of fits — nmf.cluster.flow
title: Cluster-flow (alluvial) diagram across a sequence of fits — nmf.cluster.flow
text: Visualizes hard sample clustering changes across sequence fitted models – typically model increasing ranks, also different models rank.  individual line flowing left--right across results (x-axis); vertical position result determined cluster, clusters reordered (barycenter method) reduce crossings.  Lines coloured individual's cluster reference result, one can see reference clusters split merge.  adjusted Rand index (ARI) pair adjacent results printed along top figure.  X-axis ticks default result's $rank can overridden names. Works non-negative multiplicative-update family (nmfkc, nmfae, nmfkc.net, nmfre, signed variants); hard label argmax coefficient/score matrix.

title: Cluster-flow (alluvial) diagram across a sequence of fits — nmf.cluster.flow
code: nmf.cluster.flow(fits, reference = NULL, names = NULL, plot = TRUE, ...)

title: Cluster-flow (alluvial) diagram across a sequence of fits — nmf.cluster.flow
text: fits list (length \(\ge 2\)) fitted models, \(N\) individuals.  results taken given order (sorted), may different ranks different models rank. reference index (1-based position fits) result whose clustering defines line colours.  Defaults central result, floor(length(fits) / 2) + 1 (e.g.\ 2nd 2 3 results). names Optional character vector (length length(fits)) x-axis tick labels.  Defaults result's $rank. plot Logical; draw diagram immediately calling plot.nmf.cluster.flow (default TRUE).  Set FALSE build object plot later. ... plot = TRUE, graphical arguments forwarded plot.nmf.cluster.flow (e.g.\ col, lwd, xlab, ylab, main).

title: Cluster-flow (alluvial) diagram across a sequence of fits — nmf.cluster.flow
text: object class "nmf.cluster.flow" (returned invisibly): list clusters (\(N \times R\) table: rows = individuals, columns = results, entries = cluster number = dominant-factor index fit, matches factor/basis numbering fits; factor never dominates leaves empty, unused cluster number), ypos (layout positions), ranks (result's rank), labels (x-axis labels), reference (reference index), ref.cluster (reference hard labels), ARI (adjusted Rand index pair adjacent results, length \(R - 1\)), colors (default per-individual reference colour).  Call plot (re)draw diagram.

title: Cluster-flow (alluvial) diagram across a sequence of fits — nmf.cluster.flow

title: Cross-Validation for NMF-FFB — nmf.ffb.cv
text: Performs K-fold cross-validation evaluate equilibrium mapping NMF-FFB model. fold, nmf.sem fitted training samples, yielding equilibrium mapping \(\hat Y_1 = M_{\mathrm{model}} Y_2\). held-endogenous variables \(Y_1\) predicted \(Y_2\) using mapping, mean absolute error (MAE) entries test block computed. returned value average MAE across folds. implements hyperparameter selection strategy described paper: hyperparameters chosen predictive cross-validation rather direct inspection internal structural matrices.

what: Generate a Graphviz DOT Diagram for an NMF-FFB Model — nmf.ffb.DOT
title: Generate a Graphviz DOT Diagram for an NMF-FFB Model — nmf.ffb.DOT
text: Creates Graphviz DOT script visualizes structural network estimated nmf.sem. resulting diagram displays: endogenous observed variables (\(Y_1\)), exogenous observed variables (\(Y_2\)), latent factors (\(F_1\), ..., \(F_Q\)), together non-negative path coefficients whose magnitudes exceed user-specified threshold. Directed edges represent estimated relationships: \(Y_2 \rightarrow F_q\): entries C2 (exogenous loadings), \(F_q \rightarrow Y_1\): rows X (factor--endogenous mappings), \(Y_1 \rightarrow F_q\): entries C1 (feedback paths). Edge widths scaled coefficient magnitude, nodes placed optional visual clusters. variables participating edges threshold displayed, latent factors always shown.

External Driving (Y2 -> Factor) [C2] #>   edge [color=black, fontcolor=black, style=solid]; #>   Y2_1 -> F_1 [label="0.15", penwidth=5.00]; #>  #>   // 2. what: NMF-FFB Main Estimation Algorithm (formerly NMF-SEM) — nmf.ffb
title: NMF-FFB Main Estimation Algorithm (formerly NMF-SEM) — nmf.ffb
text: Fits NMF-FFB model $$   Y_1 \approx X \bigl( \Theta_1 Y_1 + \Theta_2 Y_2 \bigr) $$ non-negativity constraints orthogonality sparsity regularization. function returns estimated latent factors, structural coefficient matrices, implied equilibrium (input–output) mapping. equilibrium, model can written $$   Y_1 \approx (- X \Theta_1)^{-1} X \Theta_2 Y_2   \equiv M_{\mathrm{model}} Y_2, $$ \(M_{\mathrm{model}} = (- X \Theta_1)^{-1} X \Theta_2\) Leontief-type cumulative-effect operator latent space. Internally, latent feedback exogenous loading matrices stored C1 C2, corresponding \(\Theta_1\) \(\Theta_2\), respectively.

title: NMF-FFB Main Estimation Algorithm (formerly NMF-SEM) — nmf.ffb
code: nmf.ffb(   Y1,   Y2,   rank = NULL,   X.init = "nndsvd",   X.L2.ortho = 100,   C1.L1 = 1,   C2.L1 = 0.1,   epsilon = 1e-06,   maxit = 5000,   seed = 123,   ... )  nmf.sem(   Y1,   Y2,   rank = NULL,   X.init = "nndsvd",   X.L2.ortho = 100,   C1.L1 = 1,   C2.L1 = 0.1,   epsilon = 1e-06,   maxit = 5000,   seed = 123,   ... )

title: NMF-FFB Main Estimation Algorithm (formerly NMF-SEM) — nmf.ffb
text: Y1 non-negative numeric matrix endogenous variables rows = variables (P1), columns = samples (N). Y2 non-negative numeric matrix exogenous variables rows = variables (P2), columns = samples (N). Must satisfy ncol(Y1) == ncol(Y2). rank Integer; number latent factors \(Q\). NULL, \(Q\) taken hidden argument ... defaults nrow(Y2). X.init Initialization strategy basis matrix X (\(P_1 \times Q\)).  One : "nndsvd" (default): Non-negative Double SVD additive randomness (NNDSVDar; Boutsidis & Gallopoulos 2008), computed internally via .nndsvdar(Y1, Q).  Requires \(Q \le \min(P_1, N)\) (-rank case falls back "runif").  Uses full SVD \(Y_1\), large \(Y_1\) consider switching "kmeans" avoid SVD memory / compute cost. "kmeans": k-means columns \(Y_1\) (samples clustered \(Q\) groups); transposed cluster centers become \(X\).  Scales well large \(Y_1\); default nmfkc. "kmeansar": "kmeans" followed filling zero entries \(X\) \(\mathrm{Uniform}(0,       \bar Y_1 / 100)\) (NNDSVDar-style additive randomness escape trivial stationary points). "runif": Uniform random entries \([0, 1]\). numeric \(P_1 \times Q\) matrix supplied user; negative entries projected 0. NULL: backward-compatible alias "nndsvd". cases result column-normalized colSums(X) = 1 iteration.  menu mirrors nmfkc's X.init option consistency across package. X.L2.ortho L2 orthogonality penalty X. controls penalty term \(\lambda_X \lVert X^\top X - \mathrm{diag}(X^\top X)   \rVert_F^2\). Default: 100. C1.L1 L1 sparsity penalty C1 (.e., \(\Theta_1\)). Default: 1.0. C2.L1 L1 sparsity penalty C2 (.e., \(\Theta_2\)). Default: 0.1. epsilon Relative convergence threshold objective function. Iterations stop relative change reconstruction loss falls value. Default: 1e-6. maxit Maximum number iterations multiplicative updates. Default: 5000 (matches nmfkc MU functions package). seed Random seed used initialize X, C1, C2. Default: 123. ... Additional hidden arguments controlling optional feedforward baseline (used \(X\) warm-start reference SC.map, input-output structural fidelity defined Satoh (2025) §4.SC.map): nmfkc.baseline Controls whether feedforward nmfkc(Y1, = Y2) fit used baseline. Possible values: Default (given) — nmf.sem runs nmfkc internally X.init string method ("nndsvd", "kmeans", ...) NULL, forwarding X.init, X.L2.ortho, epsilon, maxit, seed.  fitted \(X\) baseline used warm-start nmf.sem MU iterations, SC.map computed.  means nmf.sem(Y1, Y2, rank = Q) runs end--end without prior nmfkc call. TRUE — , force internal nmfkc call even X.init user-supplied matrix (matrix overridden). FALSE — opt ; internal call, SC.map = NA (pre-v0.6.8 behavior). nmfkc result (list $X $C) — use baseline directly (internal call); also adopted X.init latter string / NULL. M.simple Optional \(P_1 \times P_2\) pre-computed baseline mapping.  Takes precedence nmfkc.baseline SC.map calculation affect warm-start. Q Backward-compat alias rank.

title: NMF-FFB Main Estimation Algorithm (formerly NMF-SEM) — nmf.ffb
text: list components: X Estimated basis matrix (\(P_1 \times Q\)). C1 Estimated latent feedback matrix (\(\Theta_1\), \(Q \times P_1\)). C2 Estimated exogenous loading matrix (\(\Theta_2\), \(Q \times P_2\)). XC1 Feedback matrix \(X \Theta_1\). XC2 Direct-effect matrix \(X \Theta_2\). XC1.radius Spectral radius \(\rho(X \Theta_1)\). XC1.norm1 Induced 1-norm \(\lVert X \Theta_1 \rVert_{1,\mathrm{op}}\). Leontief.inv Leontief-type inverse \((- X \Theta_1)^{-1}.\) M.model Equilibrium mapping \(M_{\mathrm{model}} = (- X \Theta_1)^{-1} X \Theta_2\). amplification Latent amplification factor \(\lVert M_{\mathrm{model}} \rVert_{1,\mathrm{op}} /          \bigl\lVert X \Theta_2 \bigr\rVert_{1,\mathrm{op}}\). amplification.bound Geometric-series upper bound \(1 / (1 - \lVert X \Theta_1 \rVert_{1,\mathrm{op}})\) \(\lVert X \Theta_1 \rVert_{1,\mathrm{op}} < 1\), otherwise Inf. Q Effective latent dimension used fit. SC.cov Correlation sample model-implied covariance (flattened) \(Y_1\).  See second-moment fidelity Satoh (2025). SC.map Correlation equilibrium operator \(M_{\mathrm{model}}\) feedforward baseline mapping \(M_{\mathrm{simple}} = X_0 \Theta_0\), computed baseline supplied via M.simple nmfkc.baseline ...; otherwise NA.  See input-output structural fidelity Satoh (2025). MAE Mean absolute error \(Y_1\) equilibrium prediction \(\hat Y_1 = M_{\mathrm{model}} Y_2\). objfunc Vector reconstruction losses per iteration. objfunc.full Vector penalized objective values per iteration. iter Number iterations actually performed.

what: Statistical inference for NMF-FFB via X-fixed full pair bootstrap — nmf.ffb.inference
title: Statistical inference for NMF-FFB via X-fixed full pair bootstrap — nmf.ffb.inference
text: nmf.sem.inference performs statistical inference structural coefficient matrices \(C_1\) (latent feedback, \(\Theta_1\)) \(C_2\) (exogenous loading, \(\Theta_2\)) fitted nmf.sem model. procedure full pair bootstrap holds basis matrix \(\hat X\) original fit fixed across replicates (avoids label switching gives clean conditional interpretation: “uncertainty structural coefficients given measurement model”): replicate \(b = 1, \dots, B\), resample column indices \((i_1, \dots, i_N)\) replacement \(\{1, \dots, N\}\) form \(Y_1^{(b)} = Y_1[, ]\), \(Y_2^{(b)} = Y_2[, ]\). Re-estimate \((C_1^{(b)}, C_2^{(b)})\) running nmf.sem multiplicative updates \(X = \hat X\) held fixed (\(X\) update; centroid sort), using C1.L1, C2.L1 original fit. Discard replicates violate stationarity (\(\rho(X C_1^{(b)}) \ge 1\)) amplification ratio exceeding geometric-series bound 1\ \(C_1, C_2 \ge 0\) non-negative construction, exact zeros essentially never observed bootstrap distribution. Significance assessed via support rate small display threshold \(\delta\) (default 0.01): $$   \mathrm{sup}(c) \;=\; \frac{1}{|\mathrm{valid}|}     \sum_{b \\mathrm{valid}} \mathbf{1}\!\left( \hat c^{(b)} > \delta \right). $$ one-sided counterpart classical \(p\)-value: large support_rate indicates strong evidence entry meaningfully positive.  Significance markers follow lavaan convention natural correspondence \(p = 1 - \mathrm{sup}\): * (sup > 0.95), ** (sup > 0.99), *** (sup > 0.999).  Cutoffs use strict greater-rule mirrors standard R convention p-values (p < 0.05 / 0.01 / 0.001 → //), translated support_rate via \(\mathrm{sup} = 1 - p\).

title: Statistical inference for NMF-FFB via X-fixed full pair bootstrap — nmf.ffb.inference
code: nmf.ffb.inference(   object,   Y1,   Y2,   B = 1000L,   threshold = 0.01,   ci.level = 0.95,   C1.L1 = 1,   C2.L1 = 0.1,   seed = 123L,   ... )  nmf.sem.inference(   object,   Y1,   Y2,   B = 1000L,   threshold = 0.01,   ci.level = 0.95,   C1.L1 = 1,   C2.L1 = 0.1,   seed = 123L,   ... )

title: Statistical inference for NMF-FFB via X-fixed full pair bootstrap — nmf.ffb.inference
text: object fitted object returned nmf.sem.  Must contain X, C1, C2. Y1 Endogenous variable matrix (P1 x N).  Must match data used nmf.sem(). Y2 Exogenous variable matrix (P2 x N).  . B Number bootstrap replicates.  Default 1000; required *** threshold (sup > 0.999).  Reduce 500 exploratory speed (* / ** stay reliable). threshold Display threshold \(\delta\) support rate \(\Pr_{\mathrm{boot}}(\hat c^{(b)} > \delta)\).  Default 0.01; entries magnitude treated effectively zero path diagram. ci.level Confidence level percentile bootstrap CI. Default 0.95. C1.L1, C2.L1 L1 sparsity penalties used original nmf.sem fit.  must match fit's hyperparameters bootstrap estimate correct model.  Defaults (1.0, 0.1) match nmf.sem's defaults pass actual values used. seed Base RNG seed bootstrap.  replicate uses seed + b (resampling) seed + 1000 + b (\(C_1, C_2\) initialization).  Default 123. ... Hidden options: epsilon Convergence tolerance inner fixed-X MU loop.  Default 1e-6. maxit Maximum iterations inner MU loop. Default 5000. ncores Number parallel workers.  Default 1 (serial).  Cross-platform: uses parallel::mclapply Linux/macOS parallel::parLapply (PSOCK cluster) Windows. print.trace Logical, print progress.  Default FALSE.

C1.support.rate, C2.support.rate Per-element support rates (Q x P1 Q x P2 matrices). C1.ci.lower, C1.ci.upper, C2.ci.lower, C2.ci.upper Per-element percentile CI bounds. what: Heuristic Variable Splitting for NMF-FFB — nmf.ffb.split
title: Heuristic Variable Splitting for NMF-FFB — nmf.ffb.split
text: Infers heuristic partition observed variables exogenous (\(Y_2\)) endogenous (\(Y_1\)) blocks use NMF-FFB. method based positive-SEM logic, causal ordering, optional sign alignment using first principal component (PC1). procedure: internally standardizes variables (mean 0, sd 1), optionally flips signs variables align positively PC1, infers causal ordering repeatedly regressing variable remaining ones selecting variable largest minimum standardized coefficient, determines exogenous block scanning ordering upstream stopping first variable whose strongest parent coefficient exceeds threshold. n.exogenous supplied, overrides automatic threshold rule.

(Default: 0.1) auto.flipped Logical; TRUE, applies PC1-based automatic sign flipping standardization ensure consistent orientation. (Default: TRUE) verbose Logical; TRUE, prints progress messages resulting variable split. (Default: FALSE)

title: Element-wise Cross-Validation for nmfae (Wold's CV) — nmfae.ecv
text: nmfae.ecv performs k-fold element-wise cross-validation randomly holding individual elements \(Y_1\), assigning weight 0 via Y1.weights, evaluating reconstruction error held-elements. method (also known Wold's CV) suitable determining optimal rank pair \((Q, R)\) three-layer NMF. rank rank.encoder accept vector inputs. rank.encoder = NULL (default), rank.encoder set equal rank pairs evaluated element-wise (.e., \((Q_1, R_1), (Q_2, R_2), \dots\)). rank.encoder explicitly specified, combinations rank rank.encoder evaluated via expand.grid.

Default NULL, sets rank.encoder = rank evaluates element-wise pairs. explicitly specified, combinations rank evaluated. ... Additional arguments passed nmfae (e.g., epsilon, maxit). Also accepts: nfolds (number folds, default 5; div also accepted), seed (integer seed, default 123). backward compatibility, Q R accepted aliases rank rank.encoder.

title: Three-Layer Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF-AE) — nmfae
text: nmfae fits three-layer nonnegative matrix factorization model \(Y_1 \approx X_1 \Theta X_2 Y_2\), \(X_1\) decoder basis (column sum 1), \(\Theta\) bottleneck parameter matrix, \(X_2\) encoder basis (row sum 1), \(Y_2\) input matrix. Y2 = Y1, model acts non-negative autoencoder. Y1 != Y2, acts heteroencoder. Initialization uses three-step NMF procedure via nmfkc: (1) nmfkc(Y1, rank=Q) obtain \(X_1\), (2) nmfkc(Y1, =Y2, rank=Q) fixed \(X_1\) obtain \(C = \Theta X_2\), (3) nmfkc(Y2, rank=R) factor \(C\) \(\Theta\) \(X_2\).

title: Three-Layer Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF-AE) — nmfae
code: nmfae(   Y1,   Y2 = Y1,   rank = 2,   rank.encoder = rank,   epsilon = 1e-04,   maxit = 5000,   verbose = FALSE,   ... )

title: Three-Layer Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF-AE) — nmfae
text: Satoh, K. (2025). Applying Non-negative Matrix Factorization Covariates Multivariate Time Series. Japanese Journal Statistics Data Science.

title: Three-Layer Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF-AE) — nmfae
text: Y1 Output matrix \(Y_1\) (P1 x N). Non-negative. May contain NAs (handled via Y1.weights). Y2 Input matrix \(Y_2\) (P2 x N). Non-negative. Default Y1 (autoencoder). rank Integer. Rank decoder basis \(X_1\) (P1 x Q). Default 2. backward compatibility, Q accepted via .... rank.encoder Integer. Rank encoder basis \(X_2\) (R x P2). Default rank. backward compatibility, R accepted via .... epsilon Positive convergence tolerance. Default 1e-4. maxit Maximum number multiplicative update iterations. Default 5000. verbose Logical. TRUE, prints progress messages fitting. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments: Y1.weights Optional non-negative weight matrix (P1 x N) vector \(Y_1\), analogous weights argument lm.  Loss becomes \(\sum W_{ij} \, (Y_{1,ij} - \hat Y_{1,ij})^2\) (lm()-style, linear \(W\)).  Logical matrices (TRUE / FALSE) also accepted. Typical ECV / CV usage passes binary mask \(W \\{0,1\}\) held-elements; real-valued weights importance weighting also supported.  Default: Y1 NA, binary mask auto-generated (0 NA, 1 elsewhere). C.L1 L1 regularization parameter \(C\). Default 0. X1.L2.ortho L2 orthogonality regularization \(X_1\) columns. Default 0. X2.L2.ortho L2 orthogonality regularization \(X_2\) rows. Default 0. seed Integer seed reproducibility. Default 123. print.trace Logical. TRUE, prints progress. Default FALSE.

title: Three-Layer Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF-AE) — nmfae

Y1hat Fitted values \(X_1 \Theta X_2 Y_2\) (P1 x N). rank Named integer vector c(Q, R). objfunc Final objective value. objfunc.iter Objective values iteration. r.squared \(\mathrm{cor}(Y, \widehat Y)^2\) (Pearson; \([0,1]\)). r.squared.uncentered Uncentered \(R^2 = 1 - \|Y - \widehat Y\|_F^2 / \|Y\|_F^2\) (baseline = zero matrix). r.squared.centered Row-mean centered \(1 - \|Y - \widehat Y\|_F^2 / \|Y - \bar Y_{p\cdot}\|_F^2\). niter Number iterations performed. runtime Elapsed time difftime object. n.missing Number missing (zero-weighted) elements \(Y_1\). n.total Total number elements \(Y_1\) (P1 x N).
